original_abstract:
The escalating interest in alternative medicine has fueled significant research into dietary medicinal plants as sustainable sources of therapeutic compounds. This study aimed to isolate and characterize polyphenolic polymers from eight indigenous plants—*Mentha longifolia, Mentha arvensis, Tinospora cordifolia, Cymbopogon citratus, Foeniculum vulgare, Cassia absus, Camellia sinensis,* and *Trachyspermum ammi*—and evaluate their comprehensive biological activities, including antioxidant, antitumor, antidiabetic, and antimicrobial potential.

Phytochemical screening confirmed the presence of alkaloids, phenols, and flavonoids across the species. UHPLC profiling identified twenty-five distinct polyphenol complexes, categorized as phenolic acids, flavonoids, and alkaloids. *Camellia sinensis* exhibited the highest total phenolic and flavonoid content, with 3-feroylquinic acid identified as a major constituent (1,302 mg/L). Significant concentrations of rosmarinic, vanillic, chlorogenic, and gallic acids, along with rutin and quercetin, were also quantified across the extracts. FTIR analysis revealed active functional groups (O–H, C–O–C, and N–H) suggesting the presence of stable polyphenolic polymers conjugated with proteins and carbohydrates, which likely enhance their bioavailability.

Biological assays demonstrated robust functional properties. All extracts exhibited potent, dose-dependent antioxidant activity in DPPH, hydroxyl, nitric oxide, and superoxide radical scavenging assays. In antidiabetic tests, *M. arvensis, C. sinensis,* and *F. vulgare* significantly inhibited α-amylase and α-glucosidase enzymes. Cytotoxicity assays against Caco-2 colon cancer cells identified *T. ammi* and *C. citratus* as possessing the strongest antitumor potential. Furthermore, *F. vulgare* and *C. sinensis* displayed broad-spectrum antibacterial activity against *S. aureus, B. cereus, E. coli,* and *S. enterica*.

Principal component analysis (PCA) confirmed a distinct separation of the plants based on their functional profiles, highlighting a strong correlation between polyphenolic content and health-promoting activities. These findings validate the ethnomedicinal use of these species and underscore their potential as natural reserves for the development of functional foods and novel pharmaceutical agents in human nutrition.
humanized_abstract:
The increasing interest in complementary and alternative medicine (CAM) activities and the resultant focus on dietary medicinal plants as renewable sources of medicinal molecules have triggered substantial investigations on dietary medicinal plants. Hence, the present investigation was aimed at assessing polyphenolic polymers isolated from the crude extracts of eight locally available plant species viz., *Mentha longifolia* (FLMO), *Mentha arvensis* (FLMA), *Tinospora cordifolia* (FLTC), *Cymbopogon citratus* (FLCC), *Foeniculum vulgare* (FLFV), *Cassia absus* (FLCA), *Camellia sinensis* (FLCS) and *Trachyspermum ammi* (FLTRA) for evaluating their overall biological activities comprising of antioxidant, anticancer, antidiabetic and antimicrobial studies.

Phytochemical screening indicated the presence of alkaloids, phenols and flavonoids in all the species. The UHPLC analysis revealed the presence of twenty-five polyphenolic complexes consisting of phenolic acids, flavonoids and alkaloids. C. sinensis had the highest amount of total phenolics and flavonoids along with 3-feroylquinic acid (1,302 mg/L) followed by rutin and quercetin along with rosmarinic, vanillic, chlorogenic and gallic acids. FTIR revealed the presence of functional groups (O–H, C–O–C and N–H) indicating the occurrence of stable polyphenolic polymers that may be protein-carbohydrate conjugates thereby increasing their bioavailability.

In in vitro biological assays, all plant extracts under investigation showed retention of their in vivo biological functions. The extracts demonstrated a dosage-dependent free radical scavenging activity in DPPH, hydroxyl, nitric oxide, and superoxide anion assays. In the antidiabetic assays, *M. arvensis, C. sinensis* and *F. vulgare* showed significant inhibition of both alpha amylase and alpha glucosidase enzymes. In the cytotoxicity assays on Caco-2 colon cancer cells, *T. ammi* and *C. citratus* showed significant inhibition on cancer cells, indicating their potential use as anticancer agents. On the other hand, *F. vulgare* and *C. sinensis* showed remarkable activity against most of the bacterial strains such as *S. aureus, B. cereus, E. coli* and *S. enterica*.

Principal component analysis (PCA) showed a clear discrimination between the plants based on their individual functional profiles, and there was a high correlation between polyphenols and the respective biological activities. In accordance with their ethnomedicinal applications, the investigated plants may serve as valuable sources of functional food ingredients and new drugs in human nutrition.
